findings.message: N+1 query pattern: the endpoint first selects all Item.ids, then issues one session.get(Item, iid) per id inside the loop. For N items this is N+1 round trips to the database, scales linearly with table size on an unbounded input (no pagination/limit), and will exhaust the connection pool / dominate p95 latency under load — a clear performance regression against any meaningful API-latency SLO.
findings.suggestion: Fetch all names in a single query: `names = list(session.scalars(select(Item.name).order_by(Item.id)))`. Drop the intermediate id list and the per-id session.get loop entirely. If the dataset can grow unbounded, also add pagination (LIMIT/OFFSET or keyset) so the response size and query cost stay bounded.

findings.message: Unbounded result set: get_item_names selects every row in `items` with no LIMIT / pagination. Even after collapsing the N+1 into a single query, the response and memory footprint grow linearly with table size — a latent scalability/allocation hazard in a hot HTTP path, and a potential availability risk if the table grows large.
findings.suggestion: Add pagination (e.g. `limit`/`offset` query params or keyset pagination on `Item.id`) and cap the per-request page size. Return a paged envelope rather than `list[str]`.

findings.message: `from sqlalchemy import select` is imported inside the request handler. The import is cached after the first call so the steady-state cost is negligible, but it adds avoidable work on the first request and is inconsistent with the module-level imports used elsewhere (e.g. db/repository.py). Speculative micro-optimisation — flagged as low.
findings.suggestion: Move `from sqlalchemy import select` to the module-level imports at the top of routes/items.py.

findings.message: Missing `return names`: the function is annotated `-> list[str]` and declared with `response_model=list[str]`, but it falls off the end and implicitly returns `None`. FastAPI will then attempt to serialize `None` against `list[str]` and raise a ResponseValidationError on every call — i.e. the endpoint is 100% broken, which is also the worst possible performance outcome (every request is wasted work).
findings.suggestion: Add `return names` (or, per the suggestion above, just `return list(session.scalars(select(Item.name).order_by(Item.id)))`).
